children.text: Come the following – after I graduated, in September- at that time school started in September- I joined the Young Democrats with Rosie Castro. And I met Margret Castio and a lot of other people who remember me and I don't remember them.
Before we get there, did you ever discuss the books, you know like the autobiography, with the Sisters? "Oh, oh, Sister Margaret, Yes." And what sort of things did you talk about?

I told her that I thought it was surprising that a Catholic nun would give me a book about the Nation of Islam. I actually went looking for the Nation of Islam here in San Antonio. Because at that time, and I was young, I was stupid. You know, I didn't know. And I found them. They had their own little snack place or whatever they had- they use to make the zucchini bread and the carrot bread, all these nice breads. Because I wanted to join, I wanted to join the Nation of Islam because I wanted to know all about them. And I told her, and you know. She said go for it. "Go fo it." I think she knew that they were not going to let me in because I was't black, you know. But, I still went and I found them.
Where were they located?

They were on the Eastside on Houston. But you know, I could just take the bus straight down, just the bus the straight down. And uh, I forget- if they're still there I don't know. I don’t think they are. I do know because I taught in the west side- in the east side for a number of years. An there's a gentleman that still sells their little newsletter called "The World" or something like that. I use to give them quarters for the newspaper. You know and uh. It was, that was even now. I think the most recent was last year. So that was incredible, huh?
Can you tell me about- did you say you marched with the Lanier students?

I didn't march with them. I helped with the, I knew they were organizing and I helped to support. You know, some of them ended up at the Guadalupe Community Center. They didn't go to school. So they were there and I interacted with them. Edgar Lozano is one of them. Gosh! Speedy was the other one. Um, gosh, I'm trying to remember their names, Rodger...
Do you remember what their demands were?

Yes! They wanted to get access to higher education, cause they were all channeled into vocational schools. Which is what Edgewood was doing, which is what my parents did not want for us. You know, and the one thing I remember my mother saying, “Te pueden quitar todo.” They can't take your education from you. Bloom. And you're gonna go to school. And you're gonna go to school.

She worked as a seamstress at Fine Silver, they did piece work. And you know what piece work is? Where they do the little tickets, you know? And that’s how she got paid. She didn't have sick leave. She didn't have any insurance. She did not have any benefits, whatsoever. None. But she did that so she could pay for our tuition to go to Catholic school. And man, when I think about all she put up with. You know, years! I mean I'm talking about. And I rememever when she- She didn't work until David was, until David went to school, I don't think. But I remember when she use to leave us behind, and me and Johnny we used to go crying like ki- little babies. You know, “Don’t leave us! Don’t leave us!” And she would drop us off at one of the neighbor’s house. She used to take care of us. You know, and so, it was just somethin'.
She must have been proud when David and you finished school?

Oh yeah yeah, she really was. That's the one thing, she made my graduations- all of them. The two graduations she went to, right when I got my masters she- she was there. My father wasn't because he had passed away, but I am sure he would have been there too. That was something.
Tell me about the time you're serving as, you're working with Joe Bernal at Guadalupe Community Center?

I was an intern, he was with I'm trying to remember the exact name, but I think it was the Mexican American- The Commission for Mexican American Affairs- because from Guadalupe we moved to a house on Buena Vista Street. And Joe was very involved in the Raza Unida Movement, you know.
What year are we talking about?
Gosh. Well, I want to say ’68-’69 all the way through the 70s. Joe still remembers me too, you know.
So he was a state senator?

He was former state senator, yeah. Co-author of the first- state’s first education- bilingual education bill. He is Dr. Bernal, now. He has been a doctor for a while now. "We're gonna interview him." Oh good. Tell him you interviewed me. He's gonna tell you. Muchacha (laughter).

The Mexican American Affairs Committee was a committee of the Texas AFL-CIO, an organization that opperated from 1951 to 1971 and represented labor interests at the state level. It's records are located at the University of Texas at Arlington.

La Raza Unida Party (RUP) was a political party formed in Crystal City, Texas in 1970. It's goals were to increase poltical respresentation for Mexican Americans as well as ecomic social conditions. It's state convention was held in San Antonio in 1971, and it's first national convention in El Paso in 1972. RUP was able to get many of its candidates elected in local positons in Kyle, Lockhart, Crystal City, and other cities in South Texas. It's records are located at the Benson Latin American Collection at the University of Texas at Austin.

The Nation of Islam (NOI) is a movement which differs substantially from traditional Sunni or Shia interpretations of the religion found in majority Muslim Countries. It was founded in 1930 by Wallace Fard Muhammad in the city of Detriot, Michigan. It promotes Black Nationalism, an ideology that advocates for self-determination and a national identity for black people, particularly in the United States. One of the most famous members of the NOI is Malcom X, who joined the movement while imprisioned between 1948 and 1952. He left it in 1964 following an ideological split.
